revision.text:
[[ja:赤城神社]] [[File:MountAkagi.jpg|thumb|270px|right|{{ill|Mount Akagi|en|Mount Akagi|qid=Q415251|lt=Mount Akagi|1=Mount Akagi}}（祭祀対象）]] [[File:Akagiyama ono.jpg|thumb|270px|The summit of Mount Akagi
In the foreground are {{ill|Ōnuma (Mount Akagi)|ja|大沼 (赤城山)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōnuma (Mount Akagi)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōnuma (Mount Akagi)|1=Ono|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11437111}} and {{ill|Daidō Akagi Shrine|ja|赤城神社 (前橋市富士見町赤城山)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daidō Akagi Shrine|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daidō Akagi Shrine|qq=}}. In the background to the right is Jizōdake.]] '''Akagi Shrine''' (赤城神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|qid=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine|1=Shinto shrine}} with the name "Akagi." It is a shrine dedicated to {{ill|Mount Akagi|en|Mount Akagi|qid=Q415251|lt=Mount Akagi|1=Mount Akagi}} in {{ill|Gunma Prefecture|en|Gunma Prefecture|qid=Q129499|lt=Gunma Prefecture|1=Gunma Prefecture}}. == Overview == Akagi Shrine is a shrine that worships Mount Akagi, located at the northwestern edge of the {{ill|Kantō Plain|en|Kantō Plain|qid=Q1124865|lt=Kantō Plain|1=Kantō Plain}}. It originates from {{ill|Mountain worship|en|Mountain worship|qid=Q93697439|lt=mountain worship|1=Mountain worship}} of the mountain itself, the {{ill|caldera lake|ja|カルデラ湖|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/caldera lake|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/caldera lake|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to カルデラ|1=Caldera|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q159954}} of {{ill|Ōnuma (Mount Akagi)|ja|大沼 (赤城山)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōnuma (Mount Akagi)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōnuma (Mount Akagi)|1=Ono|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11437111}} and Konuma, as well as Jizōdake, a volcanic cone. It is said that there are about 300 Akagi Shrines across Japan, mainly in the {{ill|Kantō region|en|Kantō region|qid=Q132480|lt=Kantō region|1=Kantō region}}. Among these, the {{ill|Miyosawa Akagi Shrine|ja|赤城神社 (前橋市三夜沢町)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miyosawa Akagi Shrine|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miyosawa Akagi Shrine|qq=}} on the mountain's slope and the {{ill|Daidō Akagi Shrine|ja|赤城神社 (前橋市富士見町赤城山)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daidō Akagi Shrine|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daidō Akagi Shrine|qq=}} at the summit are considered the main shrines. == The Myōjin Taisha "Akagi Shrine" == {{OSM Location map|coord={{coord|36.4587|N|139.1655|E|qq=}}|fullscreen-option=1|zoom=10|float=right|width=280|height=250|caption=|title=Myōjin Taisha "Akagi Shrine" Debate|mark-coord1={{coord|36|29|02.6|N|139|10|39.7|E|qq=}}|label1=Miyosawa
Akagi Shrine|label-pos1=bottom|mark-size1=8|mark-coord2={{coord|36|33|10.1|N|139|11|0.5|E|qq=}}|label2=Daidō
Akagi Shrine|label-pos2=bottom|mark-size2=8|mark-coord3={{coord|36|22|2|N|139|10|3.5|E|qq=}}|label3=Ninomiya
Akagi Shrine|label-pos3=top|mark-size3=8|qq=}} {{GeoGroup|qq=}} In the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=}}, compiled in the 5th year of {{ill|Engi|ja|延長 (日本)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engi|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 延長 (元号)|1=Enchō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q1327274}} (927), Akagi Shrine is listed as a {{ill|Myōjin Taisha|ja|名神大社|fr|Myōjin Taisha|zh|名神大社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myōjin Taisha|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myōjin Taisha|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} under the name "Kōzuke Province, Setagun, Akagi Shrine." The following three shrines are considered candidates: * {{ill|Daidō Akagi Shrine|ja|赤城神社 (前橋市富士見町赤城山)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daidō Akagi Shrine|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daidō Akagi Shrine|qq=}} ({{ill|Maebashi|en|Maebashi|qid=Q201613|lt=Maebashi|1=Maebashi}}, Gunma Prefecture, {{ill|Fujimimachi Akagiyama|ja|富士見町赤城山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fujimimachi Akagiyama|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fujimimachi Akagiyama|1=Fujimi-machi Akagisan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q22125907}}, {{coord|36|33|10.10|N|139|11|0.56|E|region:JP-10_type:landmark|位置|name=Daidō Akagi Shrine|qq=}}) - Former {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社|1=Gō-sha|qid=Q134917316|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q712534}}{{efn|District Shrines|qid=Q90139226|lt=Ken-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gōsha|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gōsha|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q712534}}. * {{ill|Miyosawa Akagi Shrine|ja|赤城神社 (前橋市三夜沢町)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miyosawa Akagi Shrine|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miyosawa Akagi Shrine|qq=}} (Maebashi City, {{ill|Miyosawa Town|ja|三夜沢町|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miyosawa Town|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miyosawa Town|1=Miyosawamachi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q22125617}}, {{coord|36|29|03.94|N|139|10|41.08|E|region:JP-10_type:landmark|位置|name=Miyosawa Akagi Shrine|qq=}}) - Former {{ill|Prefectural Shrine|ja|県社|1=Ken-sha|qid=Q90139226|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q712534}}{{efn|Ken-sha|qid=Q90139226|lt=Ken-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prefectural Shrine|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prefectural Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q712534}}. * {{ill|Ninomiya Akagi Shrine|ja|二宮赤城神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninomiya Akagi Shrine|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninomiya Akagi Shrine|qq=}} (Maebashi City, {{ill|Ninomiya Town|ja|二之宮町|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninomiya Town|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninomiya Town|1=Ninomiyamachi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q28682347}}, {{coord|36|22|02.08|N|139|10|03.56|E|region:JP-10_type:landmark|位置|name=Ninomiya Akagi Shrine|qq=}}) - Former Gō-sha. The historical background and identification debates regarding these shrines are detailed in the section [[#History]] and in the individual articles. File:Akagi-jinja (Fujimi) haiden.JPG|{{ill|Daidō Akagi Shrine|ja|赤城神社 (前橋市富士見町赤城山)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daidō Akagi Shrine|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daidō Akagi Shrine|qq=}} File:Akagi-jinja (Miyosawa) haiden.JPG|{{ill|Miyosawa Akagi Shrine|ja|赤城神社 (前橋市三夜沢町)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miyosawa Akagi Shrine|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miyosawa Akagi Shrine|qq=}} File:Ninomiya-akagi-jinja shaden.JPG|{{ill|Ninomiya Akagi Shrine|ja|二宮赤城神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninomiya Akagi Shrine|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninomiya Akagi Shrine|qq=}} == History == === Establishment === [[File:Akagi-jinja (Miyosawa) hituishi.JPG|thumb|200px|right|Hitsuishi ({{ill|Miyosawa Akagi Shrine|ja|赤城神社 (前橋市三夜沢町)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miyosawa Akagi Shrine|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miyosawa Akagi Shrine|qq=}})
A ritual site centered around an iwakura.]] [[File:Akagi-jinja (Fujimi) enkei.JPG|thumb|200px|right|Distant view of {{ill|Daidō Akagi Shrine|ja|赤城神社 (前橋市富士見町赤城山)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daidō Akagi Shrine|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daidō Akagi Shrine|qq=}}
Mirrors used in rituals were discovered in the lake.]] The origins of the belief system and the establishment of the individual shrines are unclear. The name of the mountain itself is recorded in the {{ill|Man'yōshū|en|Man'yōshū|qid=Q834321|lt=Man'yōshū|1=Man'yōshū}} as "Kuroho no Nero."[https://web.archive.org/web/20160304220948/http://infux03.inf.edu.yamaguchi-u.ac.jp/~manyou/ver2_2/manyou_kekka2.php?kekka=14/3412 Man'yōshū Volume 14, Poem 3412] (Yamaguchi University Faculty of Education, Man'yōshū Search System). Until around the 8th century, it was referred to as "Kuroho no Nero."{{ill|Place Names of Gunma Prefecture|ja|群馬県の地名|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Place Names of Gunma Prefecture|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Place Names of Gunma Prefecture|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, section on Akagi Mountain. It is believed that the faith originated from the worship of the water source at the {{ill|Kasugawa River|ja|粕川 (群馬県)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kasugawa River|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kasugawa River|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (specifically the source at {{ill|Konuma (Mount Akagi)|ja|小沼 (赤城山)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Konuma (Mount Akagi)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Konuma (Mount Akagi)|1=Konuma (Mount Akagi)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q133818666}}), the thunder deity of the highest peak, Kurobisan, and {{ill|Mountain worship|en|Mountain worship|qid=Q93697439|lt=mountain worship|1=Mountain worship}} of Mount Akagi itself.{{ill|Gunma Prefectural Encyclopedia|ja|群馬県百科事典|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gunma Prefectural Encyclopedia|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gunma Prefectural Encyclopedia|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, Jōmō Shinbunsha, 1979. There is also a theory that the local powerful clan, the {{ill|Kōzuke no Fumihito|ja|上毛野氏|1=Kamitsukeno clan|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōzuke no Fumihito|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamitsukeno clan|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōzuke no Fumihito|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamitsukeno clan|qq=}}, founded the shrine.Miyagi Village Chronicle Editorial Committee, "Miyagi Village Chronicle," 1973. Various shrine traditions connect the Akagi Shrines to the Kōzuke no Fumihito. According to one theory, the name "Akagi" derives from the Kōzuke no Fumihito associating their ancestors and homeland with the "Ki" (紀, referring to {{ill|Kii Province|en|Kii Province|qid=Q864053|lt=Kii Province|1=Kii Province}}) region when compiling historical records. The ancestor's name was "Toyoki" ({{ill|Toyoki Iribiko no Mikoto|ja|豊城入彦命|1=Prince Toyokiirihiko|2=ja|3=豊城入彦命|qid=Q11633375|1=Prince Toyokiirihiko|1=Prince Toyokiirihiko|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyoki Iribiko no Mikoto|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince Toyokiirihiko|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyoki Iribiko no Mikoto|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince Toyokiirihiko|qq=}}), and the sacred mountain was named "Akaki" (Akagi Mountain). Additionally, from the expression "Karayashiro" in a poem by {{ill|Minamoto no Sanetomo|en|Minamoto no Sanetomo|qid=Q174249|lt=Minamoto no Sanetomo|1=Minamoto no Sanetomo}} in the {{ill|Kin'yō Wakashū|ja|金槐和歌集|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kin'yō Wakashū|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kin'yō Wakashū|1=Kinkai Wakashū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11647307}}, there is a theory that the shrine may have overseas origins, interpreted as "Chinese Shrine" or "Korean Shrine."Kumakura Hiroyasu, "Ancient Rulers of Eastern Japan: A Study on the Kōzuke no Fumihito" (Yūzankaku, Revised Edition, 2008). === General History === ==== Heian Period ==== According to the {{ill|Rikkokushi|en|Rikkokushi|qid=Q1151168|lt=Rikkokushi|1=Rikkokushi}}, the Akagi deity was granted divine Ranks. It was recorded in the {{ill|Shoku Nihon Kōki|en|Shoku Nihon Kōki|qid=Q1041301|lt=Zoku Nihon Kōki|1=Shoku Nihon Kōki}} in 839 (6th year of {{ill|Jōwa (Heian period)|en|Jōwa (Heian period)|qid=Q1075605|lt=Jōwa|1=Jōwa (Heian period)}}) as Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade, and later rose to Junior Fourth Rank, Upper Grade in 880 (4th year of {{ill|Gangyō|en|Gangyō|qid=Q747451|lt=Gangyō|1=Gangyō}}). The "Akagi Daimyōjin" is mentioned in various historical texts. While the precise location of the Akagi Shrine of that time is unknown, it is believed that the initial faith revolved around a local shrine (satomiya) in a village. Later, a mountain shrine (okumiya) was established at the summit. As such, it is thought that during the Ritsuryō system, the shrine was located in the village. The {{ill|Miyagi Village|ja|宮城村|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miyagi Village|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miyagi Village|1=Miyagi Village|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11453622}} chronicle suggests this satomiya may have been {{ill|Ninomiya Akagi Shrine|ja|二宮赤城神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninomiya Akagi Shrine|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninomiya Akagi Shrine|qq=}}. Another theory identifies {{ill|Santai Shrine|ja|産泰神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Santai Shrine|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Santai Shrine|1=Santai Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11575046}} (located in {{ill|Shimooyamachi|ja|下大屋町|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shimooyamachi|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shimooyamachi|1=Shimooyamachi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q28682233}}, Maebashi) as the satomiya. Currently worshipped as a deity of safe childbirth, it features a large pyroclastic rock from Mount Akagi behind the shrine's main hall. Its former south-facing approach allowed views of the mountain, indicating a primitive worship form focused on Mount Akagi.Kondō, 1996.The Miyagi Village chronicle speculates that Ninomiya Akagi Shrine was originally located here. ==== From the Kamakura to Sengoku Period ==== As the {{ill|Ritsuryō|en|Ritsuryō|qid=Q742460|lt=Ritsuryō system|1=Ritsuryō}} declined and samurai governments emerged, the village shrines (satomiya), which were supported by the {{ill|Royal court|en|Royal court|qid=Q1134237|lt=Imperial Court (Japan)|1=Royal court}} and {{ill|Kokushi (official)|en|Kokushi (official)|qid=Q1351234|lt=kokushi|1=Kokushi (official)}}, fell into decline. The center of local worship shifted to the {{ill|chūsha|ja|中社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/chūsha|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/chūsha|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (middleshrine) located at the confluence of pilgrimage routes. Meanwhile, the arrival of {{ill|Buddhism|en|Buddhism|qid=Q748|lt=Buddhism|1=Buddhism}} led to {{ill|Shinbutsu-shūgō|en|Shinbutsu-shūgō|qid=Q2279088|lt=Shinbutsu-shūgō|1=Shinbutsu-shūgō}} (syncretism of Buddhism and Shinto), with ascetics entering the mountainous regions to establish sacred training grounds. This strengthened faith in the mountain shrine (okumiya), including that of Akagi Shrine. The {{ill|Shintōshū|en|Shintōshū|qid=Q2616931|lt=Shintōshū|1=Shintōshū}} describes the origin of Akagi Daimyōjin, focusing on the shrine at the mountain summit. Buddhist influence reinterpreted the lakes and mountains as sacred entities, replacing previous views of the landscape as deities. Akagi Daimyōjin came to be represented as the "Two Daimyōjin," embodying the crater lakes {{ill|Konuma (Mount Akagi)|ja|小沼|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Konuma (Mount Akagi)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Konuma (Mount Akagi)|1=Konuma|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q6430416}} and {{ill|Onuma|en|Onuma|qid=Q11437106|lt=Ōnuma (Mount Akagi)|1=Onuma}}. Konuma was associated with {{ill|Ākāśagarbha|en|Ākāśagarbha|qid=Q416174|lt=Kokūzō Bosatsu|1=Ākāśagarbha}}, while Ōnuma was linked to {{ill|Senju Kannon|ja|千手観音|1=Thousand-armed Kannon|2=ja|3=千手観音|lt=Thousand-Armed Kannon|4=fr|5=Thousand-armed Kannon|6=ko|7=관세음보살|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senju Kannon|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senju Kannon|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}. Later, {{ill|Jizōdake|ja|地蔵岳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jizōdake|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jizōdake|1=Jizōdake|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11424674}}, the central volcanic cone, was incorporated into the faith and associated with {{ill|Kṣitigarbha|en|Kṣitigarbha|qid=Q604687|lt=Jizō Bosatsu|1=Kṣitigarbha}}, creating the concept of the "Three Deities of Akagi" (三所明神). The Senju Kannon statue of Ōnuma was enshrined at {{ill|Daidō Akagi Shrine|ja|赤城神社 (前橋市富士見町赤城山)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daidō Akagi Shrine|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daidō Akagi Shrine|qq=}}. According to the "Miyagi Village Chronicle," during the Gangyō era (880 CE), the deity of Akagi might have originally represented the god of Konuma, later joined by the deity of Ōnuma. The locations of the mountain shrine (okumiya) and the village shrine (satomiya) are believed to correspond to {{ill|Daidō Akagi Shrine|ja|赤城神社 (前橋市富士見町赤城山)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daidō Akagi Shrine|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daidō Akagi Shrine|qq=}} at Mount Akagi's Ōnuma and {{ill|Ninomiya Akagi Shrine|ja|二宮赤城神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninomiya Akagi Shrine|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninomiya Akagi Shrine|qq=}} in Ninomiya, respectively. The {{ill|chūsha|ja|中社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/chūsha|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/chūsha|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} is thought to be {{ill|Miyosawa Akagi Shrine|ja|赤城神社 (前橋市三夜沢町)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miyosawa Akagi Shrine|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miyosawa Akagi Shrine|qq=}}.Gunma Prefecture History, Volume 3. However, alternate theories suggest {{ill|Miyosawa Akagi Shrine|ja|三夜沢赤城神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miyosawa Akagi Shrine|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miyosawa Akagi Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}'s former location (Moto-Miyosawa) was the original okumiya, with a relationship between Moto-Miyosawa and Ninomiya reflected in the portable shrine procession.Ozaki, 1970. Another theory proposes Daidō as the okumiya and both Ninomiya and Miyosawa as satomiya.Imaizumi, 1974. Around the 14th century, Jizō worship was introduced, and the eastern shrine (Tōgū) of Miyosawa Akagi Shrine, dedicated to Jizō, was established at its current location. Subsequently, the western shrine (Seigū) of Moto-Miyosawa relocated to the site of the eastern shrine, forming the current two-shrine structure of Miyosawa Akagi Shrine. The western shrine is believed to be linked to Ninomiya Akagi Shrine, although its origins are unclear. During the {{ill|Sengoku Period (Japan)|ja|戦国時代|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sengoku Period (Japan)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sengoku Period (Japan)|1=Sengoku Period (Japan)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q117415390}}, Ninomiya Akagi Shrine was destroyed and fell into decline under the {{ill|Later Hōjō clan|en|Later Hōjō clan|qid=Q431928|lt=Later Hōjō clan|1=Later Hōjō clan}}. Its restoration began in the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|qid=Q184963|lt=Edo period|1=Edo period}}, under the influence of Miyosawa Akagi Shrine. Meanwhile, Daidō and Miyosawa Akagi Shrines continued to flourish under the patronage of the {{ill|Nagao clan|en|Nagao clan|qid=Q1655561|lt=Nagao clan|1=Nagao clan}} and {{ill|Uesugi clan|en|Uesugi clan|qid=Q1150058|lt=Uesugi clan|1=Uesugi clan}}. ==== Edo Period ==== In the Edo period, Daidō Akagi Shrine served as a spiritual protector of {{ill|Maebashi Castle|ja|厩橋城|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Maebashi Castle|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Maebashi Castle|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 前橋城|1=Maebashi Castle|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q10898917}} (later Maebashi Castle), particularly its {{ill|kimon|ja|鬼門|1=Kimon|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kimon|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kimon|qq=}} (northeastern direction). It gained the strong patronage of the {{ill|Maebashi Domain|en|Maebashi Domain|qid=Q10863486|lt=Maebashi Domain|1=Maebashi Domain}} lords. In 1601 (2nd year of {{ill|Keichō|en|Keichō|qid=Q1328185|lt=Keichō|1=Keichō}}), {{ill|Sakai Shigetada|ja|酒井重忠|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakai Shigetada|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakai Shigetada|1=Sakai Shigetada|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q5369481}} reconstructed the shrine. It was designated as "Shōichii Akagi Daimyōjin, Akagi Shrine," reflecting the divine Rank from the Ritsuryō era. In 1642 (19th year of {{ill|Kan'ei|en|Kan'ei|qid=Q1192913|lt=Kan'ei|1=Kan'ei}}), domain lord {{ill|Sakai Tadakiyo|en|Sakai Tadakiyo|qid=Q7402769|lt=Sakai Tadakiyo|1=Sakai Tadakiyo}} rebuilt the shrine after a fire the previous year.Fujimi Village Chronicle. Successive Maebashi lords attended the grand festivals of Daidō Akagi Shrine. During the {{ill|Kansei|en|Kansei|qid=Q1195131|lt=Kansei|1=Kansei}} era, a dispute arose between Daidō Akagi Shrine and Miyosawa Akagi Shrine over the use of titles such as "Main Shrine," "Sōsha," and "Shōichii." The dispute led to lawsuits, with notable conflicts occurring in 1798 (10th year of Kansei) and 1800 (12th year of Kansei). The resolution in 1816 (13th year of {{ill|Bunka|en|Bunka|qid=Q1373146|lt=Bunka|1=Bunka}}) mandated joint decision-making on wording and sealed titles for the shrines. ==== Post-Meiji Restoration ==== In the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|qid=Q3125096|lt=Meiji period|1=Meiji era}}, changes in religious policies led to significant transformations at Akagi Shrines. Miyosawa Akagi Shrine abolished its temple affiliations due to {{ill|Haibutsu kishaku|en|Haibutsu kishaku|qid=Q702298|lt=Haibutsu kishaku|1=Haibutsu kishaku}}, eliminating Buddhist influences. The eastern and western shrines were consolidated into a single shrine centered on the eastern shrine. At Daidō Akagi Shrine, the separation of its temple affiliation necessitated appointing new {{ill|Shinkan (official)|en|Shinkan (official)|qid=Q11589454|lt=Shinto priests|1=Shinkan (official)}} from the local community. Under the {{ill|Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|en|Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|qid=Q712534|lt=Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}}, Miyosawa was designated as a {{ill|Prefectural Shrine|ja|県社|1=Ken-sha|qid=Q90139226|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q712534}}{{efn|Ken-sha|qid=Q90139226|lt=Ken-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prefectural Shrine|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prefectural Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q712534}}, while Daidō and Ninomiya became {{ill|Village Shrines|ja|郷社|1=Gō-sha|qid=Q134917316|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q712534}}{{efn|District Shrines|qid=Q90139226|lt=Ken-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Village Shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Village Shrines|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q712534}}. Efforts to elevate Miyosawa Akagi Shrine to a {{ill|National Shrine|ja|国幣社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/National Shrine|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/National Shrine|1=National Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11420846}} in 1936 (11th year of {{ill|Shōwa era|en|Shōwa era|qid=Q327567|lt=Shōwa|1=Shōwa era}}) were interrupted by the outbreak of World War II and never realized. In 1970 (45th year of Shōwa), Daidō Akagi Shrine relocated and reconstructed its shrine buildings on Kotori-ga-shima, addressing severe weathering since their original construction in 1642. === Divine Ranks (Shinkai) === The records of divine Ranks conferred upon "Akagi no Kami" (specific shrine affiliation unknown) are as follows: * [[:en:Six National Histories|Six National Histories]] ** [[Jōwa (Japan)|Jōwa]] 6 (839), 6th month: Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade (従五位下) ({{ill|Shoku Nihon Kōki|en|Shoku Nihon Kōki|qid=Q1041301|lt=Shoku Nihon Kōki|1=Shoku Nihon Kōki}}) ** [[Jōgan (Japan)|Jōgan]] 9 (867), 6th month, 20th day: Promoted from Junior Fifth Rank, Upper Grade (従五位上) to Senior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade (正五位下) ({{ill|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|en|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|qid=Q1052981|lt=Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|1=Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku}}) ** Jōgan 11 (869), 12th month, 25th day: Senior Fifth Rank, Upper Grade (正五位上) (Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku) ** Jōgan 16 (874), 3rd month, 14th day: Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade (従四位下) (Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku) ** {{ill|Gangyō|en|Gangyō|qid=Q747451|lt=Gangyō|1=Gangyō}} 4 (880), 5th month, 25th day: Junior Fourth Rank, Upper Grade (従四位上) (Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku) – Listed as "Akagi Ishigami" (赤城石神) or "Akagi Numagami" (赤城沼神).Variants between "Akagi Ishigami" and "Akagi Numagami" appear depending on the manuscript. * After the Six National Histories ** [[Chōgen (Japan)|Chōgen]] 3 (1030, approximate): Senior First Rank (正一位) ({{ill|Kōtai Jitsuroku-chō|ja|上野国交替実録帳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtai Jitsuroku-chō|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtai Jitsuroku-chō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}) ** Senior First Rank (正一位) ({{ill|Jinmyō-chō|ja|上野国神名帳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jinmyō-chō|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jinmyō-chō|qq=}}) – Recorded as "Akagi Daimyōjin" (赤城大明神). Subsequent conferrals of divine Ranks for individual shrines are detailed in the respective articles for each shrine. == Featured Works == === {{ill|Kinpū Wakashū|ja|金槐和歌集|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kinpū Wakashū|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kinpū Wakashū|1=Kinkai Wakashū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11647307}} === * 上野の 勢多の赤城の からやしろ やまとにいかで あとをたれけむ - {{ill|Minamoto no Sanetomo|en|Minamoto no Sanetomo|qid=Q174249|lt=Minamoto no Sanetomo|1=Minamoto no Sanetomo}}（『{{ill|Kinpū Wakashū|ja|金槐和歌集|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kinpū Wakashū|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kinpū Wakashū|1=Kinkai Wakashū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11647307}}』） === {{ill|Shintōshū|en|Shintōshū|qid=Q2616931|lt=Shintōshū|1=Shintōshū}} === The {{ill|Shintōshū|en|Shintōshū|qid=Q2616931|lt=Shintōshū|1=Shintōshū}}, compiled during the {{ill|Nanboku-chō period|en|Nanboku-chō period|qid=Q264012|lt=Nanboku-chō period|1=Nanboku-chō period}}, contains origin myths of various shrines, including three stories related to this shrine.[http://akagijinja.jp/densetu/sintousyu.html Akagi Shrine Legends] (Official Website of Ōdō Akagi Shrine). * Volume 7, Story 36: "The Tale of the Ichinomiya of Kōzuke Province" :: The Ichinomiya of Kōzuke Province, Nukhoko Daimyōjin (now {{ill|Nukisaki Shrine|en|Nukisaki Shrine|qid=Q10863796|lt=Ichinomiya Nukisaki Shrine|1=Nukisaki Shrine}}), was originally a deity of foreign origin. When Akagi Daimyōjin, the former Ichinomiya, was weaving silk, raw silk became scarce, so Akagi Daimyōjin borrowed some from Nukhoko Daimyōjin. As a result, Akagi Daimyōjin yielded the title of Ichinomiya to the wealthier Nukhoko Daimyōjin, becoming a Ninomiya. * Volume 7, Story 40: "The Guardian Deity of Akagi Daimyōjin in Seta District, Kōzuke Province" :: This story narrates the tragic tale of General Takanoe no Daishō Ienari and his three daughters, who each built a shrine by the shores of Ōnuma and Konuma Lakes. * Volume 8, Story 43: "The Akagi Sansho Myōjin and Gakuman Daibosatsu of Kōzuke Province" :: This tale focuses on the monk Gakuman. It identifies the "Akagi Sansho Myōjin" as comprising Akagi Myōjin, who is worshipped at Ōnuma (associated honjibutsu: {{ill|Senju Kannon|ja|千手観音|1=Thousand-armed Kannon|2=ja|3=千手観音|lt=Thousand-Armed Kannon|4=fr|5=Thousand-armed Kannon|6=ko|7=관세음보살|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senju Kannon|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senju Kannon|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}); Konuma Myōjin, who is worshipped at Konuma (associated honjibutsu: {{ill|Ākāśagarbha|en|Ākāśagarbha|qid=Q416174|lt=Kokūzō Bosatsu|1=Ākāśagarbha}}); and Jizō-dake, where Gakuman Daibosatsu is worshipped (associated honjibutsu: {{ill|Kṣitigarbha|en|Kṣitigarbha|qid=Q604687|lt=Jizō Bosatsu|1=Kṣitigarbha}}). == List == There are 118 shrines named "Akagi Shrine" (赤城神社) in Gunma Prefecture and 334 shrines nationwide. They are spread across the Kanto region, originating from natural mountain worship or branch transfers during the Edo period. Among these, a notable example is {{ill|Akagi Shrine (Shinjuku)|ja|赤城神社 (新宿区)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akagi Shrine (Shinjuku)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akagi Shrine (Shinjuku)|1=Akagi Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11635471}} in {{ill|Akagi Motomachi|ja|赤城元町|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akagi Motomachi|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akagi Motomachi|1=Akagi-Motomachi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11635454}}, {{ill|Shinjuku|en|Shinjuku|qid=Q179645|lt=Shinjuku, Tokyo|1=Shinjuku}}. Additionally, the deities worshiped at Akagi Shrines on the southern slopes of Akagi Mountain historically showed a distinct east-west distribution. According to the Gunma Prefecture shrine registry from the early Meiji period, {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大己貴神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōkuninushi|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōkuninushi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大国主}} was worshiped in the southeast, while {{ill|Toyoki-Irihiko-no-Mikoto|ja|豊城入彦命|1=Prince Toyokiirihiko|2=ja|3=豊城入彦命|qid=Q11633375|1=Prince Toyokiirihiko|1=Prince Toyokiirihiko|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyoki-Irihiko-no-Mikoto|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince Toyokiirihiko|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyoki-Irihiko-no-Mikoto|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince Toyokiirihiko|qq=}} was venerated in the southwest. This likely originates from the {{ill|Miyosawa Akagi Shrine|ja|三夜沢赤城神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miyosawa Akagi Shrine|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miyosawa Akagi Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}'s two separate eastern and western shrines, with the Eastern Shrine dedicated to Ōkuninushi and the Western Shrine to Toyoki-Irihiko-no-Mikoto. It is believed that these eastern and western factions maintained control over their respective branch shrines. === Main Shrines === * {{ill|Akagi Shrine (Ōdō Akagi Shrine)|ja|赤城神社 (前橋市富士見町赤城山)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akagi Shrine (Ōdō Akagi Shrine)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akagi Shrine (Ōdō Akagi Shrine)|1=Daidō Akagi Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11635469}} ({{ill|Fujimi, Nagano|en|Fujimi, Nagano|qid=Q1204145|lt=Fujimi Town|1=Fujimi, Nagano}}, {{ill|Maebashi|en|Maebashi|qid=Q201613|lt=Maebashi|1=Maebashi}}, Gunma Prefecture) * {{ill|Akagi Shrine (Miyosawa Akagi Shrine)|ja|赤城神社 (前橋市三夜沢町)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akagi Shrine (Miyosawa Akagi Shrine)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akagi Shrine (Miyosawa Akagi Shrine)|1=Miyosawa Akagi Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11635467}} (Miyosawa Town, Maebashi, Gunma Prefecture) * {{ill|Ninomiya Akagi Shrine|ja|二宮赤城神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninomiya Akagi Shrine|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninomiya Akagi Shrine|qq=}} (Ninomiya Town, Maebashi, Gunma Prefecture) === Gunma Prefecture ===
